reviewer_comments.comment: Beat 7 is the weakest structural point: it is correctly framed as adversarial scoping, but the supporting set is small, recent, and only lightly connected to the main provenance line, so it can narrow claims but not credibly apportion causal weight among provenance, inference-time scaling, structured reasoning, and supervision choices. Beat 6 also sits adjacent to this weakness because it supports deployment feasibility rather than downstream effect.

Strong contradiction review overall. The pipeline surfaces major counterevidence on both independent lines, repeatedly narrows overclaims, and gives concrete handling advice instead of rhetorical certainty. It correctly attributes most tensions to the right line: collapse/web-drift/detection to pretraining, and AI-feedback/self-alignment/social-reasoning limits to fine-tuning. The main remaining weakness is cross-line handling: the danger of importing pretraining contamination evidence into fine-tuning/social-alignment conclusions is acknowledged, but not yet separated into a fully explicit contradiction track with dedicated tests and decision rules.

The gap analysis is largely honest, but it should sharpen the paper into two separable claims: (1) CampusGo is a feasible provenance-aware collection and governance system, and (2) any downstream model claim is only a pilot-level hypothesis about data-composition effects under fixed model size and fixed inference-time budget. Strengthen Beat 5 with more explicit methodology precedent for controlled fine-tuning comparisons, ablation design, benchmark validity, and statistical evaluation; right now it is correctly labeled weak, but the methodological bridge is thinner than the rest of the writeup implies. Keep Beat 6 strictly on deployment/buildability and never as evidence of model improvement. Expand Beat 7 further with competing mechanisms from inference-time scaling, structured reasoning, verifier-guided decoding, steering, and AI-feedback/self-alignment so the final thesis is explicitly narrowed to provenance-sensitive effects after holding those factors fixed. Also quantify the collapse-risk versus mitigation literature and keep the lack of a post-2022 web-scale contamination audit front and center.
["K (5/8, highest-priority shortfall; Beat 7 is supported but thin)", "F (16/17, slight core-support shortfall)", "G (11/12, slight core-support shortfall)"]

The corpus is sufficient for a cautious, scoped paper, not a sweeping causal one. It supports a motivation story about recursive contamination risk, a narrow claim that web drift is measurable but not yet shown to be globally degrading, a descriptive L_auth framework, and a primary thesis that provenance likely matters especially for socially grounded post-training tasks under fixed model size and fixed inference-time budget. It does not justify claims that all synthetic data is harmful, that the web has already broadly degraded, that L_auth is an empirically validated law, that human data is universally indispensable, that deploying CampusGo proves model gains, or that provenance effects dominate inference-time scaling and other test-time mechanisms. The pilot should be presented as directional and hypothesis-generating, with subgroup-sensitive human evaluation and provenance-controlled ablations still needed.
